sentences.text: Yes.
Right.
In Sweden, I speak Yiddish.
Very hard to communicate-- sign-- sign language.
German-- a little bit of German.
Right.
Right.
Right.
I be your pardon.
Only Yiddish and German.
And the Romanian.
The German, yes.
No.
No No.
Just-- just to each other we spoke Yiddish.
Mostly Yiddish.
Oh yeah.
The-- and the-- they reminded me in-- in Dyhernfurth the-- the camp after Auschwitz-- twice a week we were shaved like shaving on the-- on the beard-- on the face.
Three fingers, middle the head.
Twice a week we was shaved.
A sign not to run away.
And then I-- I remember another incident which I left out in first time.
I had a meister-- mei-- German meister.
Meister's a-- a-- a
A leader.
Yeah.
Conducted the foreman.
The foreman, the German foreman told-- must have been about 45 years of age-- he was the meister, the-- the foreman.
We were about 100 to 120 in his group.
And he walked around and with a stick.
And he used to-- he didn't have to-- he could just talked or told us-- hit people.
He killed people.
Just hit them in the head like we're nothing.
And the same German, the same meister [NON-ENGLISH], I got a liking to him.
He left me-- every lunch, he left me over his scrap from the lunch.
Peeled the corners from the bread, a piece of salami, a piece of whatever.
Was-- life-- was-- he was giving me life for that.
And I remember another incident.
He went away for Christmas.
And he came back after the Christmas.
And-- he went home to his family.
I'd never talked to him about his family or never.
I spoke a little of the German just from the work.
And I did my work.
I was a very good worker.
So he brought me a little package there from Christmas.
And he left it like a doggy bag in the side-- a piece of salami, cake, and some other little goodies.
It was never-- like a million dollars.
I don't-- I don't-- I don't know.
I don't know.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He was the foreman.
And he was killing people and he choose me.
He liked me.
Just-- I don't know.
I'm a good worker.
He always used to say I'm the best worker.
I worked-- I worked very, very clean, but he-- particular I didn't try to hide or to not to work and so on-- not to get hurt.
I left from the hospital in Sweden-- they cleaned me-- soap me-- the nurse-- from top to bottom.
And then I had doctors, all-- all types of doctors.
And they start give me vitamins and different medicine and very, very [INAUDIBLE] food.
They kept me in the hospital about four to six weeks I believe and-- and get to work.
And after that, they came in-- tailor took my measurement and I got-- suit of clothes.
I had two pairs of pants, a sport jacket, a couple of shirts, underwear, socks, shoes, everything-- even a little valise they gave me.
This was the pre-- they told me-- from the-- present from the government of Sweden.
They did that to a lot of people-- a lot of people-- from a-- a lot of people.
They somehow-- well, the-- from the war, they didn't suffer anything in the war and they didn't go to-- their people didn't suffer.
And they won the war.
And they said what I did, whatever-- the government in Sweden.
Very beautiful country and beautiful people.
From-- From Goteborg-- from the hospital I got all these goodies, I met some other people they are from Romania, and I got to know them in the hospital.
And one of them in particular already came-- woke up but he wasn't really normal I would say, a fellow by the name Shansin Goldstein, older than me.
And he start he didn't want to eat the meat.
It was treif.
I know what it means.
It came back to me too, and I still ate the meat.
And another guy and me and we-- three of us, we left to a camp-- they took us to a camp in Sweden in the mountains.
Kramppen.
Is that-- the name was Kramppen-- very barracks.
They had mostly young fellows like me.
They 100 to 120 young fellows that came from Bergen-Belsen or from Germany to the hospitals in Sweden.
And they-- we should put ourselves back on the feet.
And there, the-- we've we ate good.
They gave us good three meals a day, and they-- if we wanted to, after a while, we were planting trees for the government in the hills a few hours a day, four or fives hours, little trees.
You know?
And little sports, a little exercise and-- to come back to ourselves.
From there-- How long would-- did that last?
There we were-- I arrived there in '45, was liberated the 8th and-- to the 15th.
I went to Sweden May.
By June, I-- I came to the camp.
From-- in June-- another-- we lasted there about over the summer two months.
And from there-- not-- not even-- two-- two months.
Yeah.
Two months.
And we were-- barrack where we were eating, and I was always sticking to these two fellows from what we met.
We became very friendly in the hospital, in-- in Goteborg.
And they stopped eating treif, non-Koshered, meat.
And I was over at the table, and somehow I said I don't want.
I had enough.
I'm full.
I came to myself.
I stopped eating the meat.
Then another at the table-- we had another guy joined in.
And we had six-- six-- eight at the-- eight joined up with us from the 120.
And we wanted kosher.
And we didn't want anymore treif flesh-- treif food.
And we went into the leader from the-- from the camp in Sweden.
Democratic.
It's no problem.
And we said we come from religious homes.
We observe Shabbat, and we don't eat-- dietary laws and so on.
And didn't take long.
They took all eight of us and gave us cards, wallets, and put us on the train and send us away to another place in [NON-ENGLISH].
In Sweden.
And there was the Danish when they ran away from Denmark were in that hotel, religious Jews, orthodox Jews.
And there was maybe another-- I don't know how many-- 20, 30 what observing Jews in our age.
And we had main dominion.
And from there, we didn't stay there long.
And we organized, [NON-ENGLISH], another camp, a Kosher camp and they flocked in there boys and girls.
And we had a Kosher camp.
As we were in [NON-ENGLISH], in the-- in the camp, then they start coming.
Palestine was no state of Israel yet, and [NON-ENGLISH] from the Israel came from the Haganah from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH].
And they should sign us up the [NON-ENGLISH] and go to Israel.
And most of them signed up to go after the war, not go back home they decided.
And we know already what happened, the catastrophe of what their parents and their brothers and sisters got killed.
And we went on the same boat, all of us.
And there is where I find out through Israel, Tel Aviv.
I know what to do-- Jewish commander and I found out I have a brother alive in-- in Romania-- the oldest brother.
In Romania.
No.
He didn't went back.
He didn't come--
He didn't come.
He was-- he was-- he went to Bucharest just before the Hungarians came in.
And he stayed there with-- with my relatives in Bucharest.
He was cut off from us.
So when we came to Auschwitz, we-- he didn't came to Auschwitz.
And that's the way he saved himself.
And he is the one, well, he's alive.
The rest of them, all of them died.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Sweden-- didn't go Sweden.
I was in [NON-ENGLISH].
A Koshered camp.
I beg your pardon.
In Sweden, yes.
And in Sweden, I found out through Israel that I have alive a brother in-- in--
I never went to Israel from Sweden.
Now-- and-- [NON-ENGLISH], that camp-- so they-- most of them-- young people signed up to go to Palestine. 1946.
The beginning or the middle of 19-- Beginning of '46.
I had a choice to go to Palestine or come to the United States.
They all had a choice.
I mean, most of the-- my age.
So I decided on my own I'd rather-- I'll go to United States.
Palestine-- if I got, I'll go from-- if I have a chance from the American, I'll have chance I'll go to elsewhere.
Then I decided.
I got the papers, and I came to America.
How did you pay for your trip?
How did you do that?
I didn't pay.
I know-- a school took me out.
Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH].
They paid for my trip to come to the United States.
I went to Yeshiva there.
Though my had didn't stay anymore to study.
I was-- 1946-- 18 years. 18 years of age. 18, 19.
About 19-- 18. 19, I couldn't study anymore.
So I came there.
I stayed there a couple of weeks.
And they didn't have a nearly shul what they have today.
It was build up.
A matter of fact, they made an appeal in that I remember when we arrived-- 30 of us-- that put a foundation of [INAUDIBLE].
I stayed there four weeks.
I didn't have any-- I had nobody in the United States-- no cousins, no uncles, no relatives.
I didn't speak the language.
Nobody.
And I had some [INAUDIBLE] what I found here, but not much.
And I had to belong to a Yeshiva.
All of us came as a student.
And I-- no green card, no-- so I came down to New York.
And I came to [INAUDIBLE].
Which trip are you talking about?
I went from the-- [INAUDIBLE] to New York.
The Yeshiva gave me $50 or $60 to go.
I said I want to go to New York, and they helped me.
They gave me $50 or $60 in my pocket.
I went to-- [INAUDIBLE].
To Yeshiva.
I beg your pardon.
Nope.
Walked in.
And I had supper.
The first supper I had there.
Walked in I got supper.
Didn't have to pay.
OK.
And I found out there's a woman, a [NON-ENGLISH] from my town, lives Seigel Street in Brooklyn.
And she's-- the children are married.
And she has a furnished room.
And I went in, and she rent me the room for $10 a month.
I didn't own it.
I didn't have it yet.
So I-- here-- here I got a meal.
Here I rent another room, and I didn't have my-- I traveled in that time very light.
And next day, I came into Manhattan.
I had a friend of mine, and we start talking.
How would we going to do?
He's from Pole-- a Polish guy, [NON-ENGLISH], what we going to do and have to make some money.
So you know there's a place in [INAUDIBLE].
They have neck ties.
We can buy some neck ties and sell.
And, well, I haven't got any money.
So I went into a store in [INAUDIBLE].
And I told them, look.
We just arrived.
We don't speak the language.
Speak Yiddish.
And he gave me two dozen ties on credit.
Really?
Yeah.
Two dozen ties.
So on two dozen ties, I went out.
And I sold them.
I made $20.
To-- to my friends in the Yeshiva.
Individual tie, one tie, two ties.
I paid the money $2.
I sold them for $3.
And I made $20.
I'm a big shot.
I got $20.
And that gave me encouragement, and I kept doing it.
I beg your pardon?
End up from what.
Oh.
My business.
Well, well, well, well, my business-- and then I bought 10 dozen and 20 dozen and I start selling wholesale already.
Walk into stores.
I still didn't speak the language.
Then-- and then I took a little-- there in the end of apartment house where I lived.
I rented a basement.
And I bought jackets.
And while the woman wanted to throw me out and I'm bringing in all the little visitors here what for the-- has the merchandise.
So I rented the basement for $25.
And I start doing business wholesale from there.
Huh?
I beg your pardon.
Well, I have-- [NON-ENGLISH] have a family.
And I have eight grandchildren.
I beg your pardon.
Well, I had-- I had-- I had in 1952, I had three stores-- '54.
One was on Broadway and 72nd Street.
And one I had on Columbus and 61st Street.
And one I had [INAUDIBLE].
No.
No.
I lived still in Brooklyn.
And I had three stores, And I-- I run three stores with general merchandise.
Never failed.
I always paid my dues.
I've always paid everybody.
And I-- I had to keep my head above.
And I had a mind to open up chain-- my idea was to open up chain stores all over the United States.
And my advisors, my partners were not the right one.
Yeah.
Well, I-- I remember one thing.
I bought rolls.
I could have bought-- a penny cost [INAUDIBLE].
A penny had a fresh roll.
Now, for the penny, I bought two rolls.
So I bought the two rolls.
And if I went into [INAUDIBLE], I ate a half a dozen rolls, 20 rolls, with a soup.
And it was plenty for the supper.
And that's the way up I put myself on the feet.
Took a little doing.
My wife through a-- a blind date.
Yeah.
My [INAUDIBLE].
I-- after the camp, I had mixed feelings.
Then I became a-- very, very religious, very observant, very religious after the camp.
On my own.
Just on my own.
And then I slacked on a little bit of it.
I was a little [NON-ENGLISH] plain language.
And I came to a conclusion there's only way according to the Torah to go with religion-- being and bringing up a family was only way to go in life and to be happy.
Well, my children had Yeshiva Shaar.
They went to for high school-- elementary school, Yeshiva.
And they went to [INAUDIBLE].
No.
I got married, I lived in Queens.
Grew up in Monsey.
The first grade-- my-- for-- for the daughter started-- Marilyn, first year I send her to [INAUDIBLE] in Queens.
Then we moved to Monsey.
But it wasn't even first grade.
It was only kindergarten or something like that.
And I remember I went to the rabbi, and I said, it's too much money.
He said, you want a babysitter?
You have to pay for it.
There isn't a day.
I cannot go to sleep.
I never go to sleep before 1:00, 12:00.
I don't think so.
I don't think so it has anything affecting the children.
And I-- I didn't talk to my kids too much.
Well, I didn't want to talk about it all through the years.
And I didn't even talk to my wife too much about it.
And she was understanding.
She reads a lot.
She well aware and was not necessary my wife to talk about.
And we accepted life and we went along with the way it is.
No.
No.
I didn't want to--
Burden them with it.
It's--
Was my life.
I mean, it's--
Right.
Now I believe I should have talked more and maybe opened up more years ago, but I couldn't.
I couldn't go through what I went through today 40 years ago, 30 years ago.
I couldn't
Yeah.
I would-- I don't know if I have a message of-- of my thoughts-- I mean, the way I see things-- I believe about the state of Israel.
The life is not worth living if God [NON-ENGLISH] we should be Jews and go [NON-ENGLISH] Hitler [NON-ENGLISH].
Europe, the United States, Africa, South America, Far East.
Jews cannot live by the state of Israel.
The way I see it, it's not worth living.
If we were to have a state of Israel in 1939 and we could open up doors 6 million Jews-- women and children-- wouldn't have gone for [NON-ENGLISH] just on account they're Jewish.
That's my look on it.
And I-- I believe on it very strongly that Jews can only enjoy the United States, the freedom.
The anti-Semitism isn't there.
They don't like-- the Gentile world doesn't like us.
The anti-Semitism is in the United States and it's in Germany today.
And it's even in the countries where there is no Jews.
So therefore we Jews and the-- and the-- and all over the world have to see, like the Italians, like the Danish, like the Swedes, we're different.
Yes.
But we have to see to have a strong country, a Jewish state.
If we don't have a Jewish state, I say it to my children and my grand grandchildren-- our life is not worth living.
I'm repeating it.
The Gentile world would never, never recognize a state of Israel.
Only have they killed 6 million Jews.
They recognized.
And even now, they are trying.
They're envious.
They're looking to destroy us-- to destroy.
This is my message.
I thank you ever so much.
